nativeRange.habitat: Tropical Southeast Asian rivers and streams with rocky substrates, moderate to strong current, and mineral-rich hard, alkaline water. Brotia pagodula is restricted to the Thoungyin (Moei) River system on the Thailand-Myanmar border (a tributary of the Salween); Brotia herculea inhabits similar Thai river systems. Both species are found attached to rocks or on sandy bottoms in well-oxygenated water.

decorPreferences: sandy or fine smooth substrate (no sharp gravel that snags spiny shells)
smooth river rocks and rounded stones
low to moderate lighting (most Brotia prefer dim conditions)
avoid tight crevices that can trap spined Pagoda shells

diet.notes: Brotia snails graze on biofilm, filamentous algae, diatom algae, and detritus as their primary food source. They will also accept sinking algae wafers, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, and spinach, and occasional protein-based sinking foods. Maintaining adequate calcium and minerals — through methods such as adding a piece of cuttlebone, placing crushed coral in the filter, or using remineralised hard water — is important for shell integrity, as these snails tend to erode and pit in soft water over time.

goodWith: peaceful community fish (small tetras, rasboras, danios)
other peaceful snails (Nerite, Mystery, Tylomelania rabbit snails)
Sulawesi tankmates given the warm, hard, alkaline parameter overlap
avoidWith: snail-eating fish (pufferfish, loaches such as clown/yoyo)

summary: The Hercules Snail (Brotia herculea) and Pagoda Snail (Brotia pagodula) are ornate livebearing snails from Southeast Asian rivers. This entry covers both species and other Brotia occasionally seen in the trade. All require hard, alkaline water and are sourced mainly through specialty importers.
careNotes: This entry covers the Brotia genus as it appears in the aquarium trade, primarily two species. Brotia herculea, commonly sold as the Hercules Snail or White Hercules Snail, is the larger of the two — a heavy, ridged conical snail often colored white, cream, or banded with purple stripes, reaching roughly 7–10 cm at maturity. Brotia pagodula, sold as the Pagoda Snail or Horned Armor Snail, is smaller at roughly 3–5 cm but immediately recognizable by its striking stepped shell bearing sharp spiny protrusions. Additional Brotia species occasionally surface in the trade as well. Both species are generally available only through specialty importers rather than mainstream retail chains. Care requirements are broadly similar to those of Tylomelania rabbit snails: warm water in the range of 22–28 °C, a neutral to alkaline pH of 7.0–8.0, and hard water (GH 8–20, KH 5–15) with adequate calcium and mineral content. While the lower pH boundary is sometimes debated, a consistently alkaline tank with stable mineral levels is considered the more reliable target — soft, acidic water tends to erode and pit the shells over time. A sandy or fine smooth substrate is preferred, as sharp gravel can snag the Pagoda Snail's spiny shell and tight crevices can trap both species. Brotia are sensitive to poor water quality, including elevated ammonia, nitrite, and accumulated nitrate, as well as to copper-based medications and fertilisers. Acclimation should be carried out slowly, as sudden parameter swings can cause stress and shell damage. A tank of at least 40 litres is generally advised.
breedingNotes: Like other members of the family Pachychilidae, Brotia species are livebearers — females retain developing embryos internally and give birth to fully formed miniature snails rather than laying egg clutches. Sexes are not externally distinguishable. Stable hard, alkaline water, abundant biofilm, and the absence of snail predators such as assassin snails, loaches, and pufferfish are generally considered necessary for juveniles to survive. Unlike bladder snails or ramshorn snails, Brotia tend to reproduce slowly in captivity and are not associated with the kind of population explosions that can overwhelm a tank.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Specialty importer product page for the White Hercules Snail (Brotia herculea). Source for primary identification of the trade name, native range (Wild Thailand), conservative water parameter targets (72–78 °F, pH 6.5–7.5), 3-inch adult size, 10-gallon minimum tank, omnivorous-grazer diet, and 'not plant-safe' caution.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — Pagoda Snail
additional.notes: Designated secondary; covers the sibling species Brotia pagodula sold under this umbrella entry. Confirms Thai (Thoungyin River) provenance, 72–78 °F / pH 7.0–8.0 / advice that higher KH and GH aid shell integrity, 2-inch adult size, 10-gallon minimum tank, peaceful grazer temperament, and 'not plant safe' caution.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Brotia pagodula Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 22–26 °C (optimal 24–25 °C), pH 7.0–8.0, GH 6–20, KH 5–15, 3–5 cm adult size, 1–2 yr captive lifespan, 10 gal / ~40 L minimum tank, ovoviviparous (livebearing) reproduction with ~10–20 juveniles/yr, breeding rated difficult, family Pachychilidae, copper sensitivity, and that plants may be eaten if food is insufficient.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts — Hercules Snail (Brotia herculea)
additional.notes: Cross-checked Brotia herculea temperature 22–28 °C, pH 7.0–8.2, 3–4+ inch adult size, 20-gal minimum tank, peaceful temperament, generally shrimp- and plant-safe (in contrast with Aqua Imports' 'not plant safe' caution — the file resolves this as 'with caution'), livebearing reproduction, and copper sensitivity.
